title: Incremental Laws (of All Sorts) Undermine Repentance
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman argues that abolitionists should reject not only intrinsically unjust pro-life bills but also so-called 'righteous increments' (like Planned Parenthood defunding bills), because any incremental legislation undermines the call to legislative repentance and provides political cover for officials who refuse to immediately abolish abortion.
audiences: Pro-life Christian considering a defund Planned Parenthood bill
State legislator weighing whether to file an abolition bill
Pastor or church leader thinking through repentance and politics
Skeptic asking why abolitionists oppose seemingly harmless pro-life laws

questions.question: What is the long-standing legislative position of the Abolitionist Movement regarding which bills it supports?
questions.audience: Skeptic asking why abolitionists oppose seemingly harmless pro-life laws
questions.answer: The movement holds 'no bill but abolition,' meaning any legislation other than total and immediate abolition of abortion is treated as either intrinsically unjust or a distraction from the goal.
questions.quote: The position of the Abolitionist Movement regarding which bills we support has long been “no bill but abolition.” Any other legislation is either intrinsically unjust or a distraction from the point.

questions.question: What is a 'righteous increment' as defined in the internal abolitionist debate?
questions.answer: It refers to incremental bills that don't abolish abortion but also don't explicitly approve murder the way a 20-week ban or heartbeat bill does—a defund Planned Parenthood bill being the prototypical example.
questions.quote: incremental bills that do not abolish abortion, but also do not give approval to murder in the way a 20-week ban or heartbeat bill does. A Planned Parenthood defunding bill is one such example.

questions.question: Why does Silberman say that avoiding explicit violations of God's law isn't enough to make a bill acceptable?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian considering a defund Planned Parenthood bill
questions.answer: Because the case against pro-life incrementalism rests on more than technical compliance with God's law—it also rests on abolitionism's prophetic call for the repentance necessary to abolish abortion.
questions.quote: The problem with this line of reasoning is that its explicit violations of God’s law are not the only aspect of the case against pro-life incrementalism.

questions.question: How does Silberman characterize the injustice in typical pro-life regulatory bills?
questions.audience: Skeptic asking why abolitionists oppose seemingly harmless pro-life laws
questions.answer: He argues that bills like a pain-capable act or heartbeat bill choose an arbitrary point at which life is valuable and legally discriminate against those deemed not valuable, which is unjust partiality rooted in ageism.
questions.quote: a pain-capable unborn child protection act chooses an arbitrary point at which life is valuable and legally discriminates against those deemed not valuable. This is unjust partiality rooted in ageism, as is a heartbeat bill, 15-week ban, or any other law that instructs abortionists and parents when, where, why, and how to murder children.

questions.question: What does Silberman mean when he says 'Abolitionism is repentance'?
questions.audience: Pastor or church leader thinking through repentance and politics
questions.answer: He means that approaching a legislator with an abolition bill is an act of calling them to repentance, asking them to go in a fundamentally different direction from the path every pro-life group has urged on them.
questions.quote: When we approach a legislator requesting their support for an abolition bill, we are calling them to repentance, whatever terms we choose to put it in. We are asking them to go in a fundamentally different direction than they’ve gone before, and in a fundamentally different direction than they’ve been asked to go by every pro-life group.

questions.question: What internal struggle does Silberman expect a born-again legislator to face when called to support abolition?
questions.audience: State legislator weighing whether to file an abolition bill
questions.answer: The Spirit will press the legislator to author a just law upholding abolition, while the flesh will weigh political costs—relationships, donors, and reelection chances—and seek schemes that appease pro-life voters while dodging the abolition controversy.
questions.quote: The Spirit will be urging them to author or co-author a law upholding justice, but their flesh will be weighing the political cost, worrying about how doing something controversial like fighting to immediately abolish abortion will affect their relationships, donors, and reelection chances.

questions.question: Why does bringing a defunding bill alongside an abolition bill make abolitionists complicit in a legislator's compromise?
questions.answer: Because doing so feeds the legislator's flesh by handing them a substitute scheme labeled as good, adding the abolitionist to the impediments to that legislator's repentance.
questions.quote: we are enabling their flesh. We are providing the temptation. We have added ourselves to the list of impediments to their repentance. We have brought them abolition’s substitute scheme and told them that it’s good.

questions.question: Why is taking 'easy options off the table' essential to getting legislators to abolish abortion?
questions.audience: State legislator weighing whether to file an abolition bill
questions.answer: Silberman argues legislators will only choose the politically hardest path—abolition—when they cannot be celebrated as heroes for passing a lesser bill instead; as long as easier substitutes earn them praise, they will pursue those instead of abolition.
questions.quote: The only way to get them to do the most politically difficult thing is to take the easy options off the table. For as long as they can be celebrated for doing something instead of abolishing abortion, they will do something instead of abolishing abortion.

questions.question: What is T. Russell Hunter's quoted insight about how abortion remains legal?
questions.audience: Skeptic asking why abolitionists oppose seemingly harmless pro-life laws
questions.answer: Hunter argues that the never-ending stream of incremental pro-life victories is itself the key to keeping abortion legal, because those victories provide political cover that lets pro-life politicians stay in office while continuing to permit abortion.
questions.quote: “The key to keeping abortion legal is a never-ending stream of incremental pro-life victories.” The many pro-life bills passed each year provide political cover to the very pro-life politicians keeping abortion legal, thereby allowing them to remain in office and continue keeping abortion legal.

questions.question: Why does Silberman argue 'incremental abolitionism' inevitably becomes its own never-ending stream of victories?
questions.answer: Because there are infinite ways to regulate abortion without technically violating God's law, the same political incentives that fuel the pro-life establishment will lead 'incremental abolitionists' to chase a permanent pipeline of incremental wins, denying immediatism in practice.
questions.quote: While there are infinite ways to regulate abortion that violate God’s law, there are also an infinite amount of ways to regulate abortion without technically violating God’s law. The “incremental abolitionists” could just as easily have a never-ending stream of incremental victories as the pro-life establishment does; and because of the realities addressed in the previous section, this is where the “incremental abolitionist” position inevitably leads, even if unintentionally.

questions.question: How does Silberman rebut the argument that defunding Planned Parenthood meaningfully harms abortion providers?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian considering a defund Planned Parenthood bill
questions.answer: He notes that in the state where defunding was loudest, Planned Parenthood received only about $82,000/year from the state—a drop in the ocean they could easily fundraise back tenfold, making the bill political theater rather than real harm to opponents.
questions.quote: Were Planned Parenthood to be stripped of $82,000/year, they could fundraise off of that and get back ten times as much. We are not hurting Planned Parenthood by defunding them in a state like that. We are playing Politics 101: looking like we’re doing something without doing anything.

questions.question: Why does Silberman call platform-building through empty bills 'fundamentally anti-abolitionist'?
questions.answer: Because abolitionism demands being Christian all the way through; using empty rhetoric, empty legislation, and empty campaigning to grow contact lists and donor credibility is the way of the world and contradicts abolitionist principles.
questions.quote: This is a fundamentally anti-abolitionist mindset. Abolitionism is about being Christian all the way through. Empty rhetoric, empty legislation, and empty campaigning for the sake of building a larger platform is the way of the world. It’s politics 101. It’s not what abolitionists are about.

questions.question: How does Silberman handle the argument that Planned Parenthood will need defunding eventually anyway?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian considering a defund Planned Parenthood bill
questions.answer: He concedes the point but argues the timing matters: defund them after abortion is abolished, not while doing so undermines repentance and gives political cover to the politicians and lobbyists keeping the slaughter going.
questions.quote: Defund them after it’s abolished, not when doing so undermines repentance and gives cover to the very politicians and lobbyists keeping the slaughter going.

questions.question: What is the standard Silberman insists abolitionists must hold in legislative work?
questions.audience: State legislator weighing whether to file an abolition bill
questions.answer: He says the standard is abolition—equal protection of preborn children and the defiance of Roe—and abolitionists must not budge from it or enable legislators' refusal to repent by offering them easier things to do.
questions.quote: That means setting a standard of abolition — equal protection and the defiance of *Roe* — and not budging from it. It means not enabling the flesh of legislators in their refusal to repent by bringing them easier things to do.

questions.question: What distinction does the article's UPDATE draw between filing a defund bill and rejecting a budget that funds Planned Parenthood?
questions.audience: Pastor or church leader thinking through repentance and politics
questions.answer: Filing a standalone defund bill is treated as a likely scheme to delay abolition, while a righteous official's refusal to vote for or sign a budget that funds depraved entities like Planned Parenthood is legitimate, even when the funding amount is small.
questions.quote: The former is a likely scheme to delay abolition, the latter is not. Righteous governing officials should refuse to support budgets that include funding of depraved entities like Planned Parenthood, even if the amount being allocated is small.

questions.question: Why does Silberman highlight Pastor Blake Gideon as an example tied to the 'Repent With Us' conference theme?
questions.audience: Pastor or church leader thinking through repentance and politics
questions.answer: Gideon, the former President of Oklahoma Baptists, had written in 2019 urging SBC churches to oppose the bill to abolish abortion in Oklahoma; his public repentance and embrace of abolitionism is presented as evidence that repentance fuels further repentance and is central to the movement.
questions.quote: the pastor of EFBC is Blake Gideon, the former President of Oklahoma Baptists who wrote a letter in 2019 to every SBC church in the State asking them to oppose the bill to abolish abortion in Oklahoma. He publicly repented of doing so at last year’s Abolition Day rally and in the new documentary [*A Storm Comes Rolling Down the Plain*](https://vimeo.com/ondemand/astormcomesrolling). His repentance and embrace of abolitionism is bringing about the repentance of many others, and is going to continue doing so.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Direct companion piece laying out the systematic abolitionist case against incremental pro-life legislation that this article extends to 'righteous increments.'

related.title: Immediate, Not Gradual Abolition of Abortion
related.reason: Develops the immediatism tenet that Silberman says 'incremental abolitionism' denies.

related.title: Moving Beyond Regulationism: A Response to David Norman
related.reason: Directly engages the regulationism-vs-abolitionism divide that frames Silberman's argument against bills short of abolition.

related.title: Watch: Incremental Pro-Life Strategy Failure
related.reason: Visual companion to the 'never-ending stream of incremental pro-life victories' thesis Silberman cites from T. Russell Hunter.

related.title: Faux Abolitionism
related.reason: Critiques the 'incremental abolitionist' position Silberman names as anti-abolitionist in practice.

related.title: Fruits of Abolitionism: Is True Repentance Necessary?
related.reason: Extends the central claim that abolitionism is fundamentally about repentance, the lens Silberman uses to evaluate any bill.

related.title: Contra Klusendorf and Desiring God: Pro-Life Compromise Dooms Preborn Children
related.reason: Parallel argument that pro-life compromise legislation costs preborn lives, paralleling Silberman's case against incrementalism of any flavor.
